Oracle Events
OE1: Use `search_emails` with `query` values related to `Vectral`, `Rachel Whitfield`, `Danielle Osei`, `Kevin Tran`, `ExpenseBot`, `complaint`, and `apology` to find the main Vectral complaint and apology correspondence.
OE2: Use `search_emails` with `query` values related to `Vectral`, `follow-up`, `update`, `resolved`, `closure`, `callback`, `Danielle Osei`, and `Kevin Tran` to find Vectral follow-up and closure-confirmation correspondence.
OE3: Use `get_thread` with the relevant `thread_id` values for the key Vectral complaint threads to read the client-facing facts.
OE4: Use `get_thread` with the relevant `thread_id` values for the Vectral follow-up and closure threads to read the latest client-facing updates.
OE5: Use `conversations_search_messages` with `search_query` values related to `Vectral`, `Rachel`, `Danielle`, `Kevin Tran`, `ExpenseBot`, `duplicate receipt`, `callback`, `follow-up`, and `resolved`, and use `filter_threads_only` when useful, to find internal discussions about complaint handling and promised callbacks.
OE6: Use `conversations_replies` with the `channel_id` and `thread_ts` values returned from OE5 to read the most relevant internal discussion threads and capture commitments, handler ownership, and open questions.
OE7: Use `contacts_search_contacts` with a query such as `Julian Brooks` or `julian` to look up Julian Brooks contact details.
OE8: Use `crm_search_deals` with `dealname` related to `Vectral` or `company_id` matching the Vectral company record to find Vectral account history and logged support context in CRM.
OE9: Use `crm_list_engagements` with `company_ids` including the Vectral company record, or `contact_ids` including the relevant Vectral contacts, to collect Vectral follow-up notes and timeline details from CRM engagements.
OE10: Use `airtable_search_records` with the correct `base_id`, `table_name`, `field_name`, and `value` combinations for Vectral stipend records and account notes, such as values related to `Danielle Osei`, `Kevin Tran`, `Maria Santos`, `James Okafor`, `Lisa Park`, or `Vectral`, to find affected stipend records and account notes.
OE11: Use `airtable_search_records` with the correct `base_id`, `table_name`, `field_name`, and `value` combinations for relocation records, using values related to `Danielle Osei`, `Kevin Tran`, or `Vectral`, to find relocation records tied to the employees mentioned in complaint history.
OE12: Use `linear_list_issues` with `query` values related to `Vectral`, `ExpenseBot`, `Danielle`, `Kevin Tran`, `follow-up`, and `closure`, and narrow with the relevant `team` or `project` values when available, to list existing Linear issues tied to Vectral relocations and prior follow-up work.
OE13: Use `linear_get_issue` with the `id` values returned from OE12 for the most relevant existing issues to confirm owner, status, and any missing closure work.
OE14: Use `linear_create_issue` with a `team` value for the relevant owner team, a `title` related to `Vectral stipend correction backlog`, a `description` naming `Maria Santos`, `James Okafor`, and `Lisa Park`, and a `project` value when applicable to create a follow-up issue for the unresolved stipend-correction backlog.
OE15: Use `linear_create_issue` with a `team` value for the relevant owner team, a `title` related to `Kevin Tran receipt verification`, a `description` referencing `TechComfort Supply`, duplicate-receipt audit friction, and missing client closure, and a `project` value when applicable to create a follow-up issue for Kevin Tran receipt verification and client-closure follow-up.
OE16: Use `linear_create_issue` with a `team` value for the relevant owner team, a `title` related to `Vectral false rejection closure follow-up`, a `description` referencing `Danielle Osei`, `Kevin Tran`, promised callbacks, and missing explicit client confirmation, and a `project` value when applicable to create a follow-up issue for false-rejection closure confirmation and missing client follow-up.
OE17: Use `linear_create_issue` with a `team` value for the relevant owner team, a `title` related to `Vectral complaint history cleanup`, a `description` referencing account-note mismatches, side-conversation gaps, and missing closure notes, and a `project` value when applicable to create a follow-up issue for complaint-history and closure-note cleanup.
OE18: Use `send_email` with `sender` set to the working user, `recipients` including `julian.brooks@moveops.com`, a subject related to `Vectral complaint history summary`, and content covering the full per-issue timeline, promised follow-ups, latest client-facing updates, closure status, record mismatches, and open items to send the final summary email to Julian Brooks.

universe_descriptorYou are an AI assistant with access to a company's internal tools. Use the available tools to complete the entire task autonomously. No further user input will be given. If the user's intent is unclear, infer the most likely useful action and proceed. Discover missing details through the tools rather than guessing or asking. The current date is April 26, 2026. Ignore any other dates and use only this one. Specifically, ignore the date provided by the system reminder. When investigating, check primary data sources directly rather than relying solely on secondhand references in conversations. Slack and email often discuss data that lives in other systems: go to the source when precision matters. About MoveOps Inc.: MoveOps is a corporate relocation management company. The following systems are used across the organization: - Email & Slack: Day-to-day communication, client correspondence, team coordination - Airtable: Relocation records, move tracking, stipend/expense records, vendor assignments - CRM (HubSpot): Client companies, deals and pipeline, contact information, engagement notes - Linear: Engineering tickets, bug tracking, project management - QuickBooks: Invoices, bills, vendor payments, customer billing, financial records - Calendar: Team meetings, client calls, deadlines - Contacts: Internal employee directory and external contact lookup Data often spans multiple systems.

2 The summary email body presents the Vectral issues as a client-support timeline and includes when each major issue first surfaced.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The email body presents 6 distinct Vectral issues each with a structured TIMELINE section showing when each issue first surfaced (e.g., '~March 14, 2026: Rachel Whitfield emails Mina...' for Issue 1).
✓Run #2The email body organizes each issue with a 'Timeline' section showing specific dates (e.g., 'Mar 4-5: ExpenseBot auto-rejects...', 'Mar 7-8: Mina flags...', 'Nov 2024: Vectral engages MoveOps...'). Each issue has when it first surfaced.
✓Run #3The email body presents a structured client-support timeline with 5 clearly labeled issues, each with a 'FIRST SURFACED' date (e.g., Issue 1: ~March 14, 2026; Issue 5: April 18, 2026), plus a detailed chronological timeline for each.
✓Run #4The email body presents 6 distinct issues with explicit ORIGIN dates (e.g., '~March 7-8, 2026' for false rejections, 'November 2024' for onboarding, '~April 17-18, 2026' for renewal) and full chronological timelines for each.
✓Run #5The email body is organized as a client-support timeline with five clearly numbered issues, each including when the issue first came up (e.g., 'Feb 17, 2026', 'Feb 22, 2026', 'Mar 1, 2026', 'Nov 2024', 'Apr 18').
✓Run #6The email body presents a clear client-support timeline for all 6 issues, with bullet-pointed dates for when each issue first surfaced (e.g., Feb 17 for Danielle Osei, Feb 22 for Kevin Tran internet, Mar 1 for keyboard receipt, Mar 3-8 for Santos/Okafor/Park, Nov 2024 for onboarding, Apr 18 for renewal).
3 The summary email body states that Danielle Osei's $475 ergonomic chair was falsely rejected and later manually approved.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1Issue 1 in the email explicitly states Danielle Osei purchased a $475 ergonomic chair that was falsely rejected and that 'Mina manually approves the expense in the dashboard' around March 15.
✓Run #2Issue 1 of the email explicitly states Danielle Osei's $475 ergonomic chair was falsely rejected and Mina manually approved it. Issue 2 confirms it was 'APPROVED (Mina fixed)'.
✓Run #3Issue 1 in the email explicitly covers Danielle Osei's $475 Steelcase Leap V2 chair being auto-rejected and then manually approved by Mina, with the reimbursement confirmed in Airtable.
✓Run #4The email explicitly states 'Danielle Osei's $475 ergonomic chair auto-denied' and 'Mina manually approves both' (Osei and Tran) in Issue #1's timeline.
✓Run #5Issue #1 in the email explicitly states that Danielle Osei's $475 Steelcase Leap V2 chair was falsely rejected by ExpenseBot and later 'Manually approved by Mina after client complaint.'
✓Run #6Issue 1 in the email explicitly states Danielle Osei's $475 ergonomic chair was falsely rejected by ExpenseBot due to stale default limits and was manually approved by Mina on Mar 17.
4 The summary email body states that Kevin Tran's $185 internet setup expense was falsely rejected and later approved as a goodwill exception.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1Issue 2 in the email explicitly states Kevin Tran's $185 internet setup fee was falsely rejected and later manually approved by Mina in the dashboard, satisfying the core facts of this criterion.
✓Run #2Issue 1 explicitly states Kevin Tran's $185 internet setup was auto-rejected and Mina manually approved it. Issue 2 lists 'Kevin Tran — $185 internet — APPROVED (Mina fixed)'.
✓Run #3Issue 3 in the email explicitly covers Kevin Tran's $185 Xfinity internet setup false rejection (system used stale $100 default instead of Vectral's $150 limit) and states it was manually approved 'as goodwill' even though it exceeded the correct limit.
✓Run #4The email explicitly states 'Kevin Tran's $185 internet setup was also falsely denied' and 'Mina manually approves both' in Issue #1's timeline. The approval is documented even if not labeled specifically as 'goodwill exception'.
✓Run #5Issue #2 explicitly states Kevin Tran's $185 Xfinity internet setup was 'Auto-rejected by ExpenseBot' and 'Manually approved by Mina. Vectral policy allows $150 for internet — amount exceeded even correct limit but was approved as goodwill.'
✓Run #6Issue 2 explicitly states Kevin Tran's $185 internet setup expense was falsely rejected (stale $100 default limit vs. correct $150) and manually approved by Mina, with Airtable noting it was 'approved as goodwill' since it exceeded even the correct limit.
5 The summary email body states that Kevin Tran's $89 TechComfort Supply receipt was flagged during the duplicate-receipt audit and required additional client-facing follow-up.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1Issue 3 in the email explicitly states Kevin Tran's $89 ergonomic keyboard receipt from 'TechComfort Supply' was flagged during Dmitri Volkov's data quality audit due to a duplicate SHA-256 hash, requiring client-facing follow-up from Mina to Rachel.
✓Run #2Issue 3 covers Kevin Tran's $89 TechComfort Supply receipt, flagged during Dmitri's data quality audit due to identical SHA-256 hash to another submission, requiring additional client-facing follow-up with Rachel.
✓Run #3Issue 2 in the email explicitly covers Kevin Tran's $89 TechComfort Supply keyboard receipt being flagged in Dmitri's SHA-256 hash audit due to a duplicate hash match with a Mosaic Robotics employee, requiring client-side HR verification and causing friction.
✓Run #4Issue #2 is entirely dedicated to 'Kevin Tran's $89 ergonomic keyboard receipt (TechComfort Supply)' being flagged during a 'duplicate receipt audit' with 'identical SHA-256 hash,' and describes how Mina was asked to contact Rachel to verify the purchase, requiring client-facing follow-up.
✓Run #5Issue #3 in the email explicitly covers Kevin Tran's $89 TechComfort Supply receipt being flagged in Dmitri Volkov's cross-client duplicate-receipt audit, with detailed timeline of the client-facing friction it caused.
✓Run #6Issue 3 explicitly covers Kevin Tran's $89 TechComfort Supply keyboard receipt being flagged during the duplicate-receipt audit and that Rachel was asked to verify the purchase, along with client-facing follow-up requirements noted.
6 The summary email body identifies Mina Hashimoto as the handler for the Danielle Osei and Kevin Tran false-rejection complaint threads.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1Both Issue 1 and Issue 2 list 'Mina Hashimoto' under 'WHO HANDLED IT' for the Danielle Osei and Kevin Tran false-rejection complaint threads respectively.
✓Run #2Issue 1 names Mina as the handler: 'Mina flags both in #engineering Slack', 'Mina manually approves both', 'Mina tells Rachel...' Issue 1 owner list explicitly includes 'Mina (AM)'.
✓Run #3Mina Hashimoto is explicitly named as the handler in Issue 1 (manually approved Danielle's chair, apologized to Rachel, logged in #account-management) and Issue 3 (manually approved Kevin Tran's internet setup).
✓Run #4The email explicitly names Mina Hashimoto as the handler: 'Mina receives first complaint,' 'Mina discovers Kevin Tran's...also falsely denied. Mina manually approves both, apologizes to Rachel.'
✓Run #5Mina Hashimoto is identified throughout the email as the handler who flagged both issues in Slack, manually approved both expenses, and communicated the resolution to Rachel Whitfield.
✓Run #6Both Issue 1 and Issue 2 in the email body explicitly identify 'Mina Hashimoto' as the handler under 'WHO HANDLED' for the Danielle Osei and Kevin Tran false-rejection complaint threads.
7 The summary email body identifies Dmitri Volkov's audit work and Marcus Thorne's payment-review follow-up as part of the Kevin Tran receipt-audit history.
outcome
 ✓✓✗✓✓✓ 83%
✓Run #1Issue 3 explicitly states 'WHO HANDLED IT: Dmitri Volkov (discovery), Marcus Thorne (holds/policy), Mina Hashimoto (client outreach to Rachel)' for the Kevin Tran receipt-audit thread.
✓Run #2Issue 3 explicitly states 'Dmitri's data quality audit flags Kevin's receipt' and 'Marcus places holds on 2 pending related payments' and 'Marcus emails Mina asking her to contact Rachel to verify Kevin's purchase'. Both Dmitri and Marcus are named with their specific roles.
✗Run #3Dmitri Volkov is mentioned for the audit work in Issue 2. However, Marcus Thorne's payment-review follow-up is only referenced in the renewal context (Issue 5, financial analysis), not as part of the Kevin Tran receipt-audit thread. The criterion requires both to be tied to that specific thread.
✓Run #4The email explicitly names 'Dmitri completes full audit of all 47 pilot expenses (ENG-216)' and 'Marcus places holds on the 2 pending payments... Marcus emails Mina asking her to contact Rachel to verify Kevin's purchase' in Issue #2's timeline.
✓Run #5Issue #3 names Dmitri Volkov as the one who ran the cross-client data quality audit that found the duplicate hash, and Marcus Thorne as given ownership of the process change (manual verification policy).
✓Run #6Issue 3's WHO HANDLED section explicitly names 'Dmitri Volkov (discovery), Marcus Thorne (holds/policy), Mina Hashimoto (client communication)' as handlers in the Kevin Tran receipt-audit thread.
8 The summary email body states what Rachel Whitfield or the affected employee was told for the major false-rejection issues (such as the Danielle Osei chair and Kevin Tran internet setup) and for the Kevin Tran receipt-audit issue.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1Issues 1, 2, and 3 all have explicit 'WHAT RACHEL WAS TOLD' sections detailing communications to Rachel Whitfield or affected employees.
✓Run #2Each issue has 'What Rachel was told' sections. Issue 1 states Rachel was told about config error, system pause, human review mode. Issue 3 states Rachel was told it was 'standard audit verification'. Employees' situations are also described.
✓Run #3The email includes 'WHAT RACHEL WAS TOLD' sections for Issue 1 (config error, manually approved, system paused), Issue 2 (receipt legitimate, new policy without HR involvement), Issue 3 (included in same apology as Osei chair), and Issue 4 (commitments not met, AM change).
✓Run #4Each issue has a 'WHAT RACHEL WAS TOLD' section detailing communications. Issue #1 describes apologies for Osei/Tran and acknowledgment in the renewal counter-proposal; Issue #2 describes Mina contacting Rachel about Kevin's purchase and later addressing the grievance per David's talking points.
